figures.caption: Figure 4: Additional BraTS FLAIR qualitative examples. Rows are ordered from high-scoring to medium-low cases.

Table 1: Contextual comparison on 2D automatic medical image segmentation. Baseline values are paper-reported by SegDINO [Yang et al., 2025] at 256 × 256 resolution. DINO-MVR uses 512+1024 readout resolutions, but reports 2D metrics on the same 256×256 metric grid. Matchedsplit reruns would be required for strict ranking.
Table 2: Kvasir-SEG ablations under matched protocols.

Figure 2: Qualitative examples from held-out BraTS FLAIR, ISIC 2018, and Kvasir-SEG samples. Columns show the input image or slice, ground truth, PCA visualization of frozen DINO features, DINO-MVR prediction, and error map. In the error map, green denotes overlap between prediction and ground truth, red denotes false positives, and blue denotes false negatives.
Figure 1: DINO-MVR method overview. Frozen DINOv3 features are read out by scale-specific MLP probes and fused across test-time and resolution views.
Figure 6: Additional Kvasir-SEG qualitative examples. Rows are ordered from high-scoring to medium-low cases.

Figure 3: BraTS FLAIR K-patient learning curve. Points show mean performance over patientsampling seeds, with error bars denoting one standard deviation when multiple seeds are available. The square marker and dashed line denote the 40-patient reference readout.
Figure 5: Additional ISIC 2018 qualitative examples. Rows are ordered from high-scoring to medium-low cases.
